metadata.llm_reasoning: The symptom service (frontend) depends directly on the listed services. In this topology, a fault originating in any of these dependencies could propagate to frontend, making them plausible downstream fault sources. No other upstream or alternate propagation paths exist in the given topology beyond the frontend-to-dependency links, so the suspected_downstream hypothesis is consistent with the structure. The blast radius includes all dependencies, which are all critical to monitor given frontend relies on them. While multiple downstream services are viable root causes, the topology clearly supports propagation from any dependency into frontend.

result.synthesis_reasoning: All three sources show no direct error signals in the observed window (Log Agent: no observable errors; 0.00 confidence; TCB-RCA centers on frontend with zero temporal evidence; Topology confirms frontend depends on multiple services). Given the absence of concrete error events, we cannot decisively identify a single root cause. The most credible approach is to treat any direct frontend dependency as a potential unobserved root cause, with higher credibility given by structural topology (i.e., the possibility that latency originates in one of frontend's dependencies). We prioritize depth and structure over symptom-only signals, acknowledging the evidence is weak across all sources. If more telemetry becomes available (e.g., precise latency metrics per dependency or upstream error signals), re-evaluate to possibly elevate or collapse candidates.
